title: Coherent State Path Integral Monte Carlo
abstract: We propose a new quantum simulation method for a many body quantum liquid of identical particles at finite (non-zero) temperature. The new scheme expands the high temperature density matrix on the overcomplete set of single particles coherent states of John Rider Klauder instead of the usual plane waves as in conventional path integral methods. One is free to tune the elastic constant and/or the mass of the harmonic oscillator subtending the coherent states so as to maximize the computational efficiency of the algorithm. We prove that in the limit of an extremely stiff harmonic oscillator the results for the internal energy tends towards the correct expected values. Moreover we suggest that a stiff harmonic oscillator could allow the use of larger (imaginary) timesteps. This additional degree of freedom is the characteristic feature of our new algorithm and is not available in more conventional path integral methods.
summary: This paper introduces the Coherent State Path Integral Monte Carlo (CSPIMC) method, a new technique for simulating quantum many-body liquids at finite temperature. It replaces the standard plane-wave basis with coherent states of a harmonic oscillator, offering a tunable parameter that enhances computational efficiency. The method successfully reproduces known thermodynamic limits for kinetic energy estimators.
main_problem: To develop a novel and computationally efficient quantum simulation method for studying many-body quantum liquids at finite temperature.
main_result: The proposed Coherent State Path Integral Monte Carlo (CSPIMC) method successfully reconstructs the thermal density matrix, showing convergence to known results while offering a tunable parameter ($\xi$) that allows for larger imaginary time steps.
method: The method expands the high-temperature density matrix using the overcomplete set of single-particle coherent states of a Harmonic Oscillator, contrasting with conventional plane-wave path integral methods.
model_or_system: The system is a many-body quantum liquid of identical particles (bosons/fermions) governed by a thermal density matrix $\hat{ho} = \exp(- eta \hat{H})$. The method is tested on free particles and extended to systems like liquid Helium.
key_observables: Kinetic energy ($\langle\hat{T}angle$), potential energy ($\langle\hat{V}angle$), and the factor $\phi$ used in the kinetic energy estimator.
important_parameters: Inverse temperature ($ eta$), imaginary time step ($	au$), and the stiffness parameter ($\xi$) of the harmonic oscillator, which can be tuned.
main_assumptions: The method does not solve the Feynman sign problem for fermions, and the kinetic action approximation relies on the properties of coherent states.
figures_summary: Figures compare path snapshots between CSPIMC and standard PIMC for free particles, and tables detail the dependence of the factor $\phi$ on simulation parameters.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the CSPIMC formalism by replacing plane waves with coherent states, derives the necessary path integral elements, analyzes the limiting cases (stiff HO, thermodynamic limit), and compares results against standard PIMC methods.
why_interesting: The ability to tune the harmonic oscillator stiffness ($\xi$) to improve computational efficiency by allowing larger imaginary time steps is a significant algorithmic advancement for simulating quantum dynamics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. We show a comparison between equilibrium snapshots of real paths of a simulation for free distinguishable particles with our coherent states kinetic energy density matrix (left panel) and one with the standard plane waves [15] density matrix (right panel) for a test case of N = 20, m = 1, N/L3 = 0.01, T = 0.3, mh.o.ω = 0.7, M = 10 for which we found ⟨ˆT⟩= 9.0(7) with calculation (3.4) with φ ≈6.5 and the exact result is ⟨ˆT⟩≈8.99999996090802053. Diﬀerent (real) paths have diﬀerent colors. In our CSPIMC the ghost paths are not shown but they dance nearby the real paths to which they are bound by the Gaussian of Eq. (2.8). A snapshot of the ghost paths is therefore very similar to the...

FIG. 2. We show a snapshot at equilibrium for the paths in the plane wave PIMC and the real paths, ghost paths, and ghost momenta for the CSPIMC for N = 16 4He atoms (m = 0.08306 ≈1/12 ˚A −2K−1) in two dimensions interacting with a Lennard-Jones pair potential with parameters [35] σ = 2.556 ˚A, ε = 10.22 K and a cutoﬀdistance rcut = 2.5 ˚A, at a density N/L2 = 0.05 ˚A −2 and a temperature T = 1.0 K with M = 250, 2ξ = 40, ϕ = 24/25 in Boltzmann statistics. Diﬀerent colors refer to diﬀerent real or ghost atoms.

FIG. 3. We show the one dimensional ζ1D(q|q′; τ, m, ξ) calculated with a Monte Carlo scheme using 107 (with the related statistical error) sampling points and choosing smaller and smaller timestep τ. All the four integrations over the ghost variables (qa, pa) and (qb, pb) were chosen in the interval [−l, l], l = 10 (with the related ﬁnite size error). On the left mh.o. = 1 on the right mh.o. = 10. Note that the slight negative values for the high mh.o. and small τ case are numerical artifacts due to the ﬁnite size, l < ∞, error.
